text: Lesson 5.1 When and how to pitch an audit. At some point during a project you're going to notice something. The client's business has more problems than the one you were hired to fix. You can see inefficiencies and workflows they haven't even asked about. You can tell the system needs more than a single automation and you'll want to say something. But you won't be sure how. Bringing up a bigger engagement feels risky. You don't want to come across as the consultant who's always trying to expand the scope. So you might just finish the project, send the invoice, and move on. That hesitation costs you. Not because pitching audits is hard, but because nobody showed you when the conversation makes sense and how to frame it so it feels natural instead of salesy. That's what this lesson is for. And by the end of it you'll know exactly when an audit is the right move, how to pitch it so the client feels helped rather than sold, and how to decide whether to charge for it. When audits make sense and when they don't, audits are relationship expansion tools. They work when trust already exists between you and the client. That trust can come from two places. Either you've delivered a successful project and the client has seen your work firsthand, or the client already knows you through their network, a referral, or a personal connection, and they trust your judgment before any work begins. If neither of those is true, the audit will feel premature. A cold prospect who reached out about a specific reporting problem doesn't want to hear, let's step back and audit your operations. They want the reporting problem fixed and they'll find someone else who's willing to just fix it. This maps directly to the two paths you already know. Cold leads start with a project. The project builds trust. Trust opens the door to an audit. Warm leads can start with an audit because the trust is already there. The distinction matters because it determines how you enter the conversation. Not whether audits work, but when. Three signals. That it's time in practice, the right moment usually announces itself. You don't have to force it. The first signal is a successful project delivery. The client has seen what you can do.
